summary: I was born in Laos in 1954, and that's where I first learned to play the qeej when I was about 14 years old. I practiced playing the instrument there until 1975, but when I left Laos, I had to leave the qeej behind. Laos is where my early memories and connection to Hmong culture through the qeej began, shaping much of my identity before I became a refugee.

I came to the United States in 2000, and it was only after arriving here that I started playing the qeej again. Life was challenging when I first arrived, and reconnecting with the qeej helped me deal with personal and family issues. The U.S. became the place where I revived my connection to this important Hmong instrument and culture after years of silence.

During my experience with the qeej, I learned that although the instrument is the same, the sound can vary depending on where you live, like in Thailand, Laos, or Vietnam. Thailand was one of the places where Hmong people lived, and the qeej there might sound a bit different from the one in Laos. This shows how our culture adapts slightly depending on the region, but the instrument remains a vital part of our identity.

Vietnam is another country where Hmong people live, and just like in Laos and Thailand, the qeej is an important cultural instrument there. Even though the qeej is the same instrument, the way it sounds can differ slightly depending on the region, including Vietnam. This reflects the shared but diverse cultural heritage of the Hmong across these countries.

The qeej is a bamboo flute that is central to Hmong culture, and I started playing it when I was 14 in Laos. It’s not just an instrument but a form of language and storytelling, used in spiritual ceremonies, funerals, entertainment, and personal enjoyment. After stopping during my time in the refugee camp and church involvement, I revived my playing when I came to the United States in 2000. I've been playing for almost 50 years and have learned from multiple teachers by listening and observing, preserving this important tradition.
